title: Theodore Roosevelt
teaser: Meet the sickly boy from New York City who transformed himself into a cowboy, a soldier, and the youngest president in American history — then used that power to stand up to giant corporations and protect hundreds of millions of acres of wild land for the rest of us.

summary.intro: Theodore Roosevelt (1858–1919) was the 26th President of the United States and one of the most extraordinary leaders the country has ever known. Born a sickly, asthmatic child in New York City, he transformed himself through sheer determination into a boxer, cowboy, soldier, and wilderness explorer. As president from 1901 to 1909, he stood up to the most powerful corporations in America, protected hundreds of millions of acres of wild land for future generations, and won the Nobel Peace Prize for ending a war between two great empires. Thunderously energetic, endlessly curious, and absolutely fearless, Teddy Roosevelt redefined what it meant to be an American president — and an American hero.

sections.title: A Sickly Boy Who Refused to Give Up
sections.body: Born on October 27, 1858, in New York City, Theodore Roosevelt came from a wealthy family — but his childhood was anything but easy. Severe asthma made it hard for him to breathe, and he spent many nights struggling to sleep. His father sat up with him and told him plainly: 'Theodore, you have the mind but not the body. You must make your body.' Young Theodore took those words to heart. He built a small gym in his home and threw himself into boxing, wrestling, and hiking — gradually transforming himself from a frail boy who could barely climb stairs into a young man who could hike all day without tiring. He studied at Harvard, devoured books on natural history, and developed a love of wildlife that would stay with him for life. The lesson Roosevelt learned in childhood — that any weakness can be overcome through hard work and willpower — became the guiding philosophy of everything he ever did.

sections.title: Cowboy, Explorer, and Rough Rider
sections.body: In February 1884, tragedy struck Roosevelt twice in one day: his wife Alice and his mother both died within hours of each other. Devastated, he fled to the Badlands of Dakota Territory, where he became a working cowboy, cattle rancher, and deputy sheriff. The wide-open West healed him. He chased outlaws, hunted big game, and slept under the stars — building the rugged outdoorsman identity that would become his lifelong trademark. When the Spanish-American War broke out in 1898, Roosevelt resigned his post as Assistant Secretary of the Navy and helped organize the 1st U.S. Volunteer Cavalry — the famous 'Rough Riders.' Leading a remarkable mix of cowboys, Native Americans, and Ivy League athletes, he charged up Kettle Hill at the Battle of San Juan Heights in Cuba. The charge made him a national hero overnight, and he rode that fame all the way to the White House.

sections.title: The Youngest President in History
sections.body: On September 6, 1901, President William McKinley was shot by an assassin at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York. He died eight days later, and Vice President Theodore Roosevelt was sworn in as the 26th President of the United States. At 42 years old, Roosevelt became — and remains — the youngest person ever to hold the office. He immediately made clear that his presidency would be unlike anything before it. He invited Booker T. Washington to dine at the White House, declared that every American deserved a 'Square Deal' — fairness in the face of powerful special interests — and set about using the power of the presidency in bold new ways. After serving out McKinley's term, Roosevelt won the 1904 election by the largest popular vote margin of any presidential candidate in history up to that point.

sections.title: Trust Buster — Standing Up to Big Business
sections.body: By 1900, a handful of enormously powerful corporations — called 'trusts' — controlled entire American industries. Railroad monopolies, oil empires, and steel giants set whatever prices they wanted, crushed smaller competitors, and paid workers as little as possible. Roosevelt believed these trusts had grown too powerful to be challenged by any ordinary citizen — but that a president could fight back. He used the Sherman Antitrust Act to file suits against 44 different corporations, breaking up some of the most powerful monopolies in American history. His first great victory came in 1904, when the Supreme Court ordered the massive Northern Securities railroad monopoly to be dissolved. When Upton Sinclair's shocking novel The Jungle (1906) exposed the filthy conditions inside Chicago's meatpacking plants, Roosevelt sent inspectors to confirm the findings and pushed through the Meat Inspection Act and the Pure Food and Drug Act that same year. Ordinary Americans finally had a president in their corner.

sections.title: Protector of America's Wild Places
sections.body: Of all Roosevelt's achievements, perhaps the most lasting is what he did for America's wilderness. Deeply shaped by his years on the frontier and his lifelong love of wildlife, he believed that America's forests, rivers, and wild places were a priceless inheritance — one that could be destroyed forever if not protected. During his presidency, he established 150 national forests, 51 federal bird reserves, 5 national parks, and 18 national monuments, protecting approximately 230 million acres in total — an area larger than the entire state of Texas. He created the U.S. Forest Service in 1905 and appointed Gifford Pinchot as its first chief. In 1903, he camped for three days in Yosemite Valley with the legendary naturalist John Muir, who helped inspire him to go even further. In 1908, he designated the Grand Canyon a national monument, telling critics: 'Leave it as it is. You cannot improve on it.' Today, the conservation legacy Roosevelt built is often called 'America's best idea.'

keyFacts: Theodore Roosevelt was the youngest president in U.S. history, taking office at age 42 after President McKinley was assassinated in 1901.
He protected approximately 230 million acres of American land, creating 150 national forests, 5 national parks, and 18 national monuments.
Roosevelt was the first American ever to win the Nobel Peace Prize, awarded in 1906 for negotiating the end of the Russo-Japanese War.
His 'Rough Riders' regiment famously charged up Kettle Hill in Cuba during the Spanish-American War — mostly on foot, not on horseback.
The teddy bear is named after Roosevelt, after a famous 1902 cartoon showed him refusing to shoot a bear that had been tied to a tree during a hunting trip.

videos.title: Biography of Theodore Roosevelt for Kids: Meet the American President for Kids - FreeSchool
videos.description: FreeSchool's narrated biography covers Roosevelt's life from his sickly childhood and time as a cowboy through his presidency, trust-busting, and conservation legacy — a thorough, calm introduction well-suited as a first overview of the topic.

videos.title: Theodore Roosevelt: EPIC Mini Biography | Learning History in 2 Minutes
videos.description: A fast-paced two-minute overview of Roosevelt's most important achievements — from the Rough Riders charge to the Nobel Peace Prize — perfect as a fun warm-up before diving into the full topic.

videos.title: Who Was Theodore Roosevelt? | All About Theodore Roosevelt for Kids | Twinkl USA
videos.description: Twinkl's teacher-created video covers Roosevelt's key accomplishments — from overcoming childhood asthma to becoming president and protecting America's wilderness — in clear, kid-friendly language perfect for ages 6–12.

readings.title: Who Was Theodore Roosevelt?
readings.author: Michael Burgan
readings.citationChicago: Burgan, Michael. Who Was Theodore Roosevelt? Illustrated by Jerry Hoare. New York: Grosset & Dunlap, 2014.
readings.summary: Part of the hugely popular Who Was? chapter-book series, this biography tells Roosevelt's full story in plain, engaging prose written for readers aged 8–12. Michael Burgan covers Roosevelt's sickly childhood, his years as a cowboy in the Dakota Badlands, his charge with the Rough Riders in Cuba, and his record-breaking presidency — complete with trust-busting and national parks. Black-and-white illustrations and a friendly tone make American history feel like an adventure story, not a textbook.

readings.title: The Camping Trip That Changed America: Theodore Roosevelt, John Muir, and Our National Parks
readings.author: Barb Rosenstock
readings.citationChicago: Rosenstock, Barb. The Camping Trip That Changed America: Theodore Roosevelt, John Muir, and Our National Parks. Illustrated by Mordicai Gerstein. New York: Dial Books for Young Readers, 2012.
readings.summary: This award-winning illustrated narrative tells the true story of the three-day camping trip in Yosemite Valley where Theodore Roosevelt and naturalist John Muir talked around a campfire under the stars — and Roosevelt came away determined to protect America's wild places forever. It brings the sights and sounds of the wilderness to life and shows how two extraordinary people can change the world just by having the right conversation at the right moment. Winner of the first Theodore Roosevelt Association Children's Book Prize.

readings.title: Theodore Roosevelt Papers — Library of Congress Digital Collection
readings.author: Library of Congress
readings.citationChicago: Library of Congress. "Theodore Roosevelt Papers." Digital Collections. https://loc.gov/collections/theodore-roosevelt-papers/about-this-collection/.
readings.summary: The Library of Congress has put more than 276,000 of Theodore Roosevelt's actual documents online — completely free — including his personal letters, diaries, speeches, and presidential papers. Older students and curious parents can read Roosevelt's own words about everything from his ranching days in the Badlands to his Nobel Peace Prize. It is one of the largest collections of original presidential documents in the world, and all of Roosevelt's own writing is in the public domain, meaning anyone can read it for free.

learnMoreImages.caption: Theodore Roosevelt in a formal portrait from approximately 1876, during his Harvard college years — just a few years after a childhood plagued by such severe asthma that he frequently struggled to breathe through the night. His father urged him to build himself up physically, setting him on the path that would transform a sickly boy into one of America's most vigorous presidents.

Theodore Roosevelt in his mountain climbing costume, photographed in the American West in 1880 — throwing himself into the vigorous outdoor life his father had challenged him to live. He built a private gym at home, took up boxing and wrestling, and drove himself into the wilderness, transforming from a frail asthmatic boy into a young man described by friends as tireless and fearless.

Colonel Roosevelt (center) and his Rough Riders at the top of the hill they captured at the Battle of San Juan, Cuba, July 1898 — photographed by William Dinwiddie, Library of Congress. The regiment was a remarkable mixture of cowboys, Native Americans, Ivy League athletes, and ranchers, united by Roosevelt's infectious energy and the shared adventure of the Spanish-American War.

Frederic Remington's famous 1898 painting depicting the Rough Riders' charge — which actually shows the assault on Kettle Hill, not San Juan Hill itself. Despite their name, most Rough Riders had been forced to leave their horses behind in Florida when transport ships ran out of space; they charged up Kettle Hill on foot, making 'Rough Riders' one of history's most courageous and misleading nicknames.

Colonel Theodore Roosevelt in his full Rough Rider military uniform, photographed by George G. Rockwood in 1898. Roosevelt called his personal philosophy 'The Strenuous Life' — the belief that physical challenge and adventure made people stronger and better. Leading men into battle in Cuba was the ultimate expression of that philosophy, and it made him a national hero overnight.

questions.prompt: What serious illness did Theodore Roosevelt suffer from as a child?
questions.explanation: Theodore Roosevelt suffered from severe asthma as a child, which made it hard for him to breathe and often kept him confined to bed. His father encouraged him to build his body through vigorous exercise and outdoor activity — a challenge Roosevelt embraced so completely that he became one of the most physically active presidents in American history.

questions.prompt: What did Roosevelt's father encourage him to do to overcome his childhood weakness?
choices: Take daily medicine prescribed by doctors
Rest indoors and avoid cold weather
Build his strength through hard physical exercise
Move to a warmer climate in the South
questions.correctAnswer: Build his strength through hard physical exercise
questions.explanation: Roosevelt's father told him, 'Theodore, you have the mind but not the body. You must make your body.' He urged young Theodore to build himself up through exercise. Roosevelt took this to heart, working out in a private gym, hiking, and throwing himself into every physical challenge he could find — transforming from a sickly boy into a famously rugged outdoorsman.

questions.prompt: What was the name of the volunteer cavalry regiment Theodore Roosevelt helped lead during the Spanish-American War in 1898?
questions.explanation: Roosevelt helped organize and lead the 1st United States Volunteer Cavalry, nicknamed the 'Rough Riders.' The regiment was a remarkable mix of cowboys, Native Americans, Ivy League athletes, and ranchers — united by their love of adventure and Roosevelt's infectious energy.

questions.prompt: The Rough Riders fought their famous battle on horseback, just as their name suggests.
questions.explanation: False! Despite their name, most of the Rough Riders had to leave their horses behind in Florida when the transport ships ran out of space. They charged up Kettle Hill at the Battle of San Juan Heights on foot — making 'Rough Riders' one of history's most misleading nicknames, but the charge was no less courageous for it.

questions.prompt: What did Theodore Roosevelt call his personal philosophy of living a vigorous, active, and adventurous life?
questions.explanation: Roosevelt called his personal philosophy 'The Strenuous Life' — the belief that hardship, adventure, and physical challenge made people stronger and better. He gave a famous speech by that name in 1899 and lived by it every day, from boxing in the White House to hunting expeditions deep in the wilderness.

learnMoreImages.caption: President Theodore Roosevelt in his official 1904 presidential portrait by the Pach Brothers studio. Roosevelt became the 26th President in September 1901 at age 42, making him — still today — the youngest person ever to assume the office. He brought to the presidency the same relentless energy that had driven him to rebuild his own body as a child.

The Temple of Music at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York, photographed by C.D. Arnold in 1901. Inside this ornate building on September 6, 1901, President William McKinley was shot by an assassin while shaking hands with the public. McKinley died eight days later, and Theodore Roosevelt — who was hiking in the Adirondack Mountains when he received the urgent message — was sworn in as president on September 14.

Theodore Roosevelt in a formal portrait by the Pach Brothers studio, taken during his presidency. Roosevelt called his domestic program the 'Square Deal' — a promise that all Americans, whether rich or poor, powerful or ordinary, would be treated fairly. The Square Deal focused on reining in powerful corporations, protecting workers and consumers, and preserving the nation's natural resources for the future.

questions.prompt: At what age did Theodore Roosevelt become President of the United States in 1901?
questions.explanation: Theodore Roosevelt became president at age 42, making him the youngest person ever to hold the office. He was serving as Vice President when President William McKinley was shot by an assassin in September 1901, and he was sworn in immediately after McKinley's death.

questions.prompt: How did Theodore Roosevelt become president in September 1901?
choices: He won a special presidential election
Congress appointed him after a vote
President McKinley was assassinated and Roosevelt, as Vice President, took office
The previous president retired and named him as his successor
questions.correctAnswer: President McKinley was assassinated and Roosevelt, as Vice President, took office
questions.explanation: President William McKinley was shot by an assassin at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York. McKinley died eight days later on September 14, 1901, and Vice President Roosevelt was sworn in as president that same day. He was hiking in the Adirondack Mountains when he received the urgent news.

questions.prompt: What did Roosevelt call his domestic policy program that promised fairness for ordinary working Americans?
questions.explanation: Roosevelt called his domestic program the 'Square Deal' — a promise that all Americans, whether rich or poor, powerful or ordinary, would be treated fairly. The Square Deal focused on controlling powerful corporations, protecting workers and consumers, and preserving the nation's natural resources.

questions.prompt: Theodore Roosevelt never won a presidential election — he only became president because McKinley was killed.
questions.explanation: False. After serving out McKinley's term, Roosevelt ran for president in the election of 1904 and won by a landslide — receiving the largest popular vote margin of any presidential candidate up to that point. He was enormously popular and won the election decisively in his own right.

questions.prompt: In what year did Theodore Roosevelt become President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Theodore Roosevelt became president on September 14, 1901, after President McKinley died from his wounds. Roosevelt served until March 1909 — first completing McKinley's term, then serving a full term of his own after winning the 1904 election.

learnMoreImages.caption: Upton Sinclair, as he appeared on the cover of Time Magazine — the same investigative journalist whose 1906 novel The Jungle exposed the horrifying, unsanitary conditions inside Chicago's meatpacking plants. Roosevelt read the book, had government inspectors confirm its findings, then used those findings to push through both the Meat Inspection Act and the Pure Food and Drug Act in 1906.

J. Pierpont Morgan photographed by the Pach Brothers studio in 1902 — one of the most powerful financiers in American history and the man behind the Northern Securities Company railroad monopoly. Roosevelt ordered his attorney general to sue to break it up under the Sherman Antitrust Act, and the Supreme Court ruled in 1904 that Northern Securities must be dissolved, sending a clear message that no business was above the law.

questions.prompt: What is a 'trust' in the context of Theodore Roosevelt's era?
choices: A government savings bank for ordinary citizens
A large company or group of companies that controls an entire industry and crushes competition
A promise politicians make to voters during an election
A type of contract between the government and small businesses
questions.correctAnswer: A large company or group of companies that controls an entire industry and crushes competition
questions.explanation: In Roosevelt's era, a 'trust' was a massive business combination that controlled an entire industry — like oil, steel, or railroads — and used that power to drive out competition and charge whatever prices they wanted. Roosevelt believed these monopolies were unfair to ordinary Americans and used the law to break the worst ones apart.

questions.prompt: What law did Roosevelt use to break up powerful business monopolies that were hurting American consumers?
choices: The Pure Food and Drug Act
questions.explanation: Roosevelt used the Sherman Antitrust Act of 1890 — a law that had been largely ignored for years — to file suits against powerful monopolies. His most famous early victory was breaking up the Northern Securities Company, a massive railroad monopoly, in 1904.

questions.prompt: What famous 1906 novel exposed the horrifying, unsanitary conditions inside American meatpacking factories and helped convince Roosevelt to act?
questions.explanation: Upton Sinclair's novel The Jungle (1906) shocked the nation with its vivid descriptions of filthy conditions inside Chicago's meatpacking plants — spoiled meat, rats, and worse. Roosevelt read the book, had government investigators confirm its findings, and pushed through the Meat Inspection Act of 1906 to clean up the industry.

questions.prompt: Theodore Roosevelt believed that all large businesses were harmful and should be broken up by the government.
questions.explanation: False. Roosevelt made an important distinction between 'good trusts' — large companies that competed fairly, paid fair wages, and treated customers honestly — and 'bad trusts' that used unfair tricks to crush competitors and exploit consumers. He targeted only the bad trusts, not big businesses in general.

questions.prompt: What was the name of the massive railroad monopoly that Roosevelt's government broke up in 1904 — his first major antitrust victory?
questions.explanation: The Northern Securities Company was a massive railroad monopoly formed by some of the most powerful financiers in America, including J.P. Morgan. Roosevelt ordered his attorney general to sue to break it up under the Sherman Antitrust Act, and the Supreme Court ruled in 1904 that the company must be dissolved — sending a clear message that no business was above the law.

learnMoreImages.caption: Gifford Pinchot photographed by Harris & Ewing in 1914 during his years as Chief of the U.S. Forest Service — the position Roosevelt created for him in 1905. Pinchot was Roosevelt's closest conservation ally, and together they believed that America's forests must be carefully managed and protected so that future generations could benefit from them, not just the timber companies of today.

President Theodore Roosevelt and Arizona Governor Alexander Brodie at the Grand Canyon, photographed by Underwood & Underwood on May 6, 1903 — the same day Roosevelt delivered a speech urging Americans to leave the canyon unchanged for future generations. Five years later, in January 1908, he used the Antiquities Act to designate the Grand Canyon a national monument, protecting it from the mining and development interests who wanted to exploit it.

President Theodore Roosevelt and naturalist John Muir photographed at Glacier Point in Yosemite Valley, 1903, by Underwood & Underwood — with Upper and Lower Yosemite Falls visible in the background. Muir — often called the 'Father of the National Parks' — spent three days camping with Roosevelt here, talking by firelight about the urgent need to protect wild places from development. The trip deeply shaped Roosevelt's entire conservation legacy.

questions.prompt: Approximately how many acres of American land did Theodore Roosevelt protect during his presidency?
questions.explanation: During his presidency, Roosevelt protected approximately 230 million acres of land — an area larger than the entire state of Texas. He created 150 national forests, 51 federal bird reserves, 5 national parks, and 18 national monuments, more than any president before him.

questions.prompt: Who was Roosevelt's close conservation ally and the first chief of the United States Forest Service?
questions.explanation: Gifford Pinchot was Roosevelt's chief forester and one of the most important figures in American conservation history. Roosevelt appointed him as the first chief of the newly created U.S. Forest Service in 1905. Together, they believed that forests and wilderness must be carefully managed and protected for future generations.

questions.prompt: Which breathtaking natural wonder did Roosevelt designate as a national monument in 1908 to protect it from mining and development?
questions.explanation: In January 1908, Roosevelt used the Antiquities Act to designate the Grand Canyon as a national monument, protecting it from mining and development. He famously said of it: 'Leave it as it is. You cannot improve on it.' It became a full national park in 1919.

questions.prompt: Theodore Roosevelt created the National Park Service, the government agency that manages all national parks today.
questions.explanation: False. Roosevelt created many national parks, monuments, and forests — but the National Park Service, the agency that manages them all, was created in 1916 under President Woodrow Wilson. Roosevelt's conservation work laid the foundation for it, but Wilson signed the law that actually established the agency.

questions.prompt: What famous naturalist and wilderness advocate camped with Roosevelt in Yosemite Valley in 1903 and helped inspire him to dramatically expand his conservation efforts?
questions.explanation: John Muir — often called the 'Father of the National Parks' — spent three days camping with Roosevelt in Yosemite Valley in 1903. Away from politicians and reporters, Muir talked with Roosevelt about the importance of protecting wild places, and the camping trip deeply influenced Roosevelt's conservation legacy.

learnMoreImages.caption: Portrait postcard of the five key figures at the Portsmouth Peace Conference, 1905: Japanese envoys Baron Komura and Takahira (left), Russian envoys Witte and Rosen (right), and President Theodore Roosevelt (center). Roosevelt's role in bringing these rival delegations together and brokering the Treaty of Portsmouth that ended the Russo-Japanese War earned him the Nobel Peace Prize in 1906 — making him the first American ever to receive the honor.

Battleships of the U.S. Atlantic Fleet saluting during their visit to Amoy, China, circa October–November 1908 — part of the Great White Fleet's around-the-world voyage that Roosevelt ordered in 1907. The world tour was the ultimate expression of his 'Big Stick' policy: the United States would negotiate diplomatically and quietly, but every nation on earth should know the full strength of America's navy stood ready behind those words.

President Theodore Roosevelt at the controls of a massive Bucyrus steam shovel during his November 1906 inspection of the Panama Canal construction site — photographed by The New York Times. The canal became possible after Roosevelt supported Panama's independence from Colombia in 1903 and the new nation granted the U.S. the right to build it. When it opened in 1914, the Panama Canal was one of the greatest engineering achievements in all of human history.

Official Nobel Prize portrait of Theodore Roosevelt, 1906 — the year he became the first American citizen ever to win the Nobel Peace Prize. The Nobel Committee honored his role in negotiating the Treaty of Portsmouth, which ended the brutal Russo-Japanese War. Roosevelt remains one of only four U.S. presidents ever to receive the Nobel Peace Prize.

questions.prompt: For what achievement did Theodore Roosevelt win the Nobel Peace Prize in 1906?
choices: He ended the Spanish-American War by negotiating with Spain
He negotiated the peace treaty that ended the Russo-Japanese War
He created the League of Nations to prevent future wars
He brokered a peace agreement between Mexico and the United States
questions.correctAnswer: He negotiated the peace treaty that ended the Russo-Japanese War
questions.explanation: Roosevelt won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1906 for his role in negotiating the Treaty of Portsmouth, which ended the brutal Russo-Japanese War in 1905. He brought Russian and Japanese diplomats to Portsmouth, New Hampshire, and helped them reach an agreement — making him the first American ever to win the Nobel Peace Prize.

questions.prompt: What did Roosevelt's famous motto 'Speak softly and carry a big stick' mean as a foreign policy approach?
choices: Always shout loudly in negotiations to show strength
Avoid all military action and rely on words alone
Be diplomatic in negotiations, but always have strong military power ready to back your words
Let other countries solve their own problems without American involvement
questions.correctAnswer: Be diplomatic in negotiations, but always have strong military power ready to back your words
questions.explanation: Roosevelt's 'Big Stick' policy meant that the United States should negotiate calmly and diplomatically — 'speak softly' — but that other nations should always know America had the military strength to enforce its position if needed. The 'big stick' was America's powerful navy and army, ready to act if diplomacy failed.

questions.prompt: How did the United States gain the right to build the Panama Canal during Roosevelt's presidency?
choices: The US purchased the canal zone from Spain after the Spanish-American War
Congress voted to seize the land directly from Colombia
France donated the canal zone to the United States as a gift
Roosevelt supported Panama's independence from Colombia, and the new nation gave the US the right to build the canal
questions.correctAnswer: Roosevelt supported Panama's independence from Colombia, and the new nation gave the US the right to build the canal
questions.explanation: Panama was part of Colombia, which refused to grant the US rights to build a canal. In 1903, Panama declared independence from Colombia — with the US Navy positioned nearby — and the new nation quickly granted the US the right to build and operate the canal. It opened in 1914 and became one of the greatest engineering achievements in history.

questions.prompt: Theodore Roosevelt was the first American citizen ever to win the Nobel Peace Prize.
questions.explanation: True. When Roosevelt won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1906 for his role in ending the Russo-Japanese War, he became the first American ever to receive the honor. He remains one of only four U.S. presidents to have won a Nobel Peace Prize.

questions.prompt: What was the name of the 1904 foreign policy declaration in which Roosevelt claimed the United States had the right to intervene in Latin American countries to keep order?
questions.explanation: The Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine (1904) declared that the United States would intervene in Latin American nations that could not manage their own affairs — essentially making the US the 'policeman' of the Western Hemisphere. It significantly expanded American power and influence in the region.
